items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.

items.q: Von Neumann's EDVAC report describing stored-program computers dates from:
items.why: Correct answer: 1945. “1936” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie's UNIX was first released in:
items.why: It's 1969. “1965” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Von Neumann's EDVAC report was circulated?
items.why: The real figure is 1945 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
